## TOWARDS A UNIFIED VIEW OF PARAMETER-EFFICIENT TRANSFER LEARNING

## **Junxian He** _[∗]_

Carnegie Mellon University junxianh@cs.cmu.edu

**Chunting Zhou** _[∗]_ Carnegie Mellon University chuntinz@cs.cmu.edu

## **Xuezhe Ma**

University of Southern California xuezhema@isi.edu

**Taylor Berg-Kirkpatrick** UC San Diego tberg@eng.ucsd.edu

**Graham Neubig** Carnegie Mellon University gneubig@cs.cmu.edu

## ABSTRACT

Fine-tuning large pretrained language models on downstream tasks has become the de-facto learning paradigm in NLP. However, conventional approaches finetune all the parameters of the pretrained model, which becomes prohibitive as the model size and the number of tasks grow. Recent work has proposed a variety of parameter-efficient transfer learning methods that only fine-tune a small number of (extra) parameters to attain strong performance. While effective, the critical ingredients for success and the connections among the various methods are poorly understood. In this paper, we break down the design of state-of-the-art parameter-efficient transfer learning methods and present a unified framework that establishes connections between them. Specifically, we re-frame them as modifications to specific hidden states in pretrained models, and define a set of design dimensions along which different methods vary, such as the function to compute the modification and the position to apply the modification. Through comprehensive empirical studies across machine translation, text summarization, language understanding, and text classification benchmarks, we utilize the unified view to identify important design choices in previous methods. Furthermore, our unified framework enables the transfer of design elements across different approaches, and as a result we are able to instantiate new parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods that tune less parameters than previous methods while being more effective, achieving comparable results to fine-tuning all parameters on all four tasks.[1]

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Transfer learning from pre-trained language models (PLMs) is now the prevalent paradigm in natural language processing, yielding strong performance on many tasks (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2020). The most common way to adapt general-purpose PLMs to downstream tasks is to fine-tune all the model parameters ( _full fine-tuning_ ). However, this results in a separate copy of fine-tuned model parameters for each task, which is prohibitively expensive when serving models that perform a large number of tasks. This issue is particularly salient with the ever-increasing size of PLMs, which now range from hundreds of millions (Radford et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020) to hundreds of billions (Brown et al., 2020) or even trillions of parameters (Fedus et al., 2021).

To mitigate this issue, a few lightweight alternatives have been proposed to update only a small number of extra parameters while keeping most pretrained parameters frozen. For example, _adapter tuning_ (Houlsby et al., 2019) inserts small neural modules called adapters to each layer of the pretrained network and only the adapters are trained at fine-tuning time. Inspired by the success of prompting methods that control PLMs through textual prompts (Brown et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021a), _prefix tuning_ (Li & Liang, 2021) and _prompt tuning_ (Lester et al., 2021) prepend an additional _l_ tunable

(2021) learn low-rank matrices to approximate parameter updates. We illustrate these methods in Figure 1. These approaches have all been reported to demonstrate comparable performance to full fine-tuning on different sets of tasks, often through updating less than 1% of the original model parameters. Besides parameter savings, parameter-efficient tuning makes it possible to quickly adapt to new tasks without catastrophic forgetting (Pfeiffer et al., 2021) and often exhibits superior robustness in out-of-distribution evaluation (Li & Liang, 2021).

However, we contend that the important ingredients that contribute to the success of these parameterefficient tuning methods are poorly understood, and the connections between them are still unclear. In this paper, we aim to answer three questions: (1) How are these methods connected? (2) Do these methods share design elements that are essential for their effectiveness, and what are they? (3) Can the effective ingredients of each method be transferred to others to yield more effective variants?

In order to answer these questions, we first derive an alternative form of prefix tuning that reveals prefix tuning’s close connections with adapters (§3.1). Based on this we then devise a unified framework that frames the aforementioned methods as different ways to modify the hidden representations of frozen PLMs (§3.2). Our unified framework decomposes previous methods along a _shared_ set of design dimensions, such as the function used to perform the modification, the position in which to impose this modification, and how to integrate the modification. This framework allows us to transfer design choices across approaches to propose new variants such as adapters with multiple heads (§3.3). In experiments, we first show that existing parameter-efficient tuning methods still lag behind full fine-tuning on higher-resource and challenging tasks (§4.2), as exemplified in Figure 2. Then we utilize the unified framework to identify critical design choices and validate the proposed variants empirically (§4.3-4.6). Our experiments on four NLP benchmarks covering text summarization, machine translation (MT), text classification, and general language understanding, demonstrate that the proposed variant uses less parameters than existing methods while being more effective, matching full fine-tuning results on all four tasks.

## 2 PRELIMINARIES

## 2.1 RECAP OF THE TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE

The transformer model (Vaswani et al., 2017) is now the workhorse architecture behind most stateof-the-art PLMs. In this section we recap the equations of this model for completeness. Transformer models are composed of _L_ stacked blocks, where each block (Figure 1) contains two types of sub-

## 2.2 OVERVIEW OF PREVIOUS PARAMETER-EFFICIENT TUNING METHODS

Below and in Figure 1, we introduce several state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning methods. Unless otherwise specified, they only tune the added parameters while the PLM’s are frozen.

**Adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019):** The adapter approach inserts small modules (adapters) between transformer layers. The adapter layer generally uses a down-projection with _**W**_ down _∈_ R _[d][×][r]_ to project the input _**h**_ to a lower-dimensional space specified by bottleneck dimension _r_ , followed by a nonlinear activation function _f_ ( _·_ ), and a up-projection with _**W**_ up _∈_ R _[r][×][d]_ . These adapters are surrounded by a residual connection, leading to a final form:

_**h** ←_ _**h**_ + _f_ ( _**hW**_ down) _**W**_ up _._ (4)

Houlsby et al. (2019) places two adapters sequentially within one layer of the transformer, one after the multi-head attention and one after the FFN sub-layer. Pfeiffer et al. (2021) have proposed a more efficient adapter variant that is inserted only after the FFN “add & layer norm” sub-layer.

**Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang, 2021):** Inspired by the success of textual prompting methods (Liu et al., 2021a), prefix tuning prepends _l_ tunable prefix vectors to the keys and values of the multihead attention at every layer. Specifically, two sets of prefix vectors _**P** k,_ _**P** v ∈_ R _[l][×][d]_ are concatenated with the original key _**K**_ and value _**V**_ . Then multi-head attention is performed on the new prefixed keys and values. The computation of head _i_ in Eq. 2 becomes:

_**P** k_ and _**P** v_ are split into _Nh_ head vectors respectively and _**P** k_[(] _[i]_[)] _[,]_ _**[ P]**_[ (] _v[i]_[)] _∈_ R _[l][×][d/N][h]_ denote the _i_ -th head vector. Prompt-tuning (Lester et al., 2021) simplifies prefix-tuning by only prepending to the input word embeddings in the first layer; similar work also includes P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021b).

**LoRA (Hu et al., 2021):** LoRA injects trainable low-rank matrices into transformer layers to approximate the weight updates. For a pre-trained weight matrix _**W** ∈_ R _[d][×][k]_ , LoRA represents its update with a low-rank decomposition _**W**_ +∆ _W_ = _**W**_ + _**W**_ down _**W**_ up, where _**W**_ down _∈_ R _[d][×][r] ,_ _**W**_ up _∈_ R _[r][×][k]_ are tunable parameters. LoRA applies this update to the query and value projection matrices ( _**W** q,_ _**W** v_ ) in the multi-head attention sub-layer, as shown in Figure 1. Further, we note that _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][h][×][l]_ and _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[l][×][d][h]_ are lowrank matrices when _l_ is small, and thus they function similarly to the _**W**_ down and _**W**_ up matrices in adapters. This view also suggests that the number of prefix vectors, _l_ , plays a similar role to the bottleneck dimension _r_ in adapters: they both represent the rank limitation of computing the modification vector ∆ _**h**_ . Thus we also refer _l_ as the bottleneck dimension. Intuitively, the rank limitation implies that ∆ _**h**_ is a linear combination of _the same l_ (or _≤ l_ ) basis vectors for any _**x**_ .

**The Difference from Adapters:** In addition to the gating variable _λ_ , we emphasize three differences between prefix tuning and adapters. (1) As demonstrated in Figure 3, prefix tuning uses _**x**_ , the input of the PLM layer, to compute ∆ _**h**_ , while adapters use _**h**_ , the output of the PLM layer. Thus, prefix tuning can be thought of as a “parallel” computation to the PLM layer, whereas the typical adapter is “sequential” computation. (2) Adapters are more flexible with respect to where they are inserted than prefix tuning: adapters typically modify attention or FFN outputs, while prefix tuning only modifies the attention output of each head. Empirically, this makes a large difference as we will show in §4.4. (3) Eq. 10 applies to each attention head, while adapters are always single-headed, which makes prefix tuning more expressive: head attention is of dimension _d/Nh_ – basically we have full rank updates to each attention head if _l ≥ d/Nh_ , but we only get full-rank updates to the whole attention output with adapters if _r ≥ d_ . Notably, prefix tuning is not adding more parameters than adapters when _l_ = _r_ .[7] We empirically validate such multi-head influence in §4.4.

## 3.2 THE UNIFIED FRAMEWORK

Inspired by the connections between prefix tuning and adapters, we propose a general framework that aims to unify several state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning methods. Specifically, we cast them as learning a modification vector ∆ _**h**_ , which is applied to various hidden representations. Formally, we denote the hidden representation to be directly modified as _**h**_ , and the direct input to the PLM sub-module that computes _**h**_ as _**x**_ (e.g. _**h**_ and _**x**_ can be the attention output and input respectively). To characterize this modification process, we define a set of design dimensions, and different methods can be instantiated by varying values along these dimensions. We detail the design dimensions below, and illustrate how adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA fall along them in Table 1:

**Functional Form** is the specific function that computes ∆ _**h**_ . We have detailed the functional form for adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA in Eq. 4, 6, and 10 respectively. The functional forms of all these methods are similar with a proj ~~d~~ own _→_ nonlinear _→_ proj ~~u~~ p architecture, while “nonlinear” degenerates to the identity function in LoRA.

**Modified Representation** indicates which hidden representation is directly modified.[8]

**Insertion Form** is how the added module is inserted into the network. As mentioned in the previous section and shown in Figure 3, traditionally adapters are inserted at a position in a sequential manner, where both the input and output are _**h**_ . Prefix tuning and LoRA – although not originally described in this way – turn out to be equivalent to a parallel insertion where _**x**_ is the input.

**Composition Function** is how the modified vector ∆ _**h**_ is composed with the original hidden representation _**h**_ to form the new hidden representation. For example, adapters perform simple additive composition, prefix tuning uses a gated additive composition as shown in Eq. 10, and LoRA scales ∆ _**h**_ by a constant factor and adds it to the original hidden representation as in Eq. 6.

We note that many other methods not present in Table 1 fit into this framework as well. For example, prompt tuning modifies the head attention in the first layer in a way similar to prefix tuning, and various adapter variants (Pfeiffer et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021) can be represented in a similar way as adapters. Critically, the unified framework allows us to study parameter-efficient tuning methods along these design dimensions, identify the critical design choices, and potentially transfer design elements across approaches, as in the following section.

## 3.3 TRANSFERRING DESIGN ELEMENTS

Here, and in Figure 3, we describe just a few novel methods that can be derived through our unified view above by transferring design elements across methods: (1) _Parallel Adapter_ is the variant by transferring the parallel insertion of prefix tuning into adapters. Interestingly, while we motivate the parallel adapter due to its similarity to prefix tuning, concurrent work (Zhu et al., 2021) independently proposed this variant and studied it empirically; (2) _Multi-head Parallel Adapter_ is a further step to make adapters more similar to prefix tuning: we apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs as prefix tuning. This way the variant improves the capacity for free by utilizing the multi-head projections as we discuss in §3.1. (3) _Scaled Parallel Adapter_ is the variant by transferring the composition and insertion form of LoRA into adapters, as shown in Figure 3e.

Our discussion and formulation so far raise a few questions: Do methods varying the design elements above exhibit distinct properties? Which design dimensions are particularly important? Do the novel methods described above yield better performance? We answer these questions next.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 GENERAL SETUP

**Datasets:** We study four downstream tasks: (1) XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) is an English summarization dataset where models predict a summary given a news article; (2) English to Romanian translation using the WMT 2016 en-ro dataset (Bojar et al., 2016); (3) MNLI (Williams et al., 2018) is an English natural language inference dataset where models predict whether one sentence entails, contradicts, or is neutral to another. (4) SST2 (Socher et al., 2013) is an English sentiment classification benchmark where models predict whether a sentence’s sentiment is positive or negative.

**Setup:** We use BARTLARGE (Lewis et al., 2020) and a multilingual version of it, mBARTLARGE (Liu et al., 2020a), as the underlying pretrained models for XSum and en-ro translation respectively, and we use RoBERTaBASE (Liu et al., 2019) for MNLI and SST2. We vary the bottleneck dimension within _{_ 1 _,_ 30 _,_ 200 _,_ 512 _,_ 1024 _}_ if needed.[9] We mainly study adapters, prefix tuning (prefix), and LoRA which greatly outperform bitfit and prompt tuning in our experiments. In the analysis sections (§4.3-4.5) we insert adapters _either_ at the attention or FFN layers for easier analysis, but include the results of inserting at both places in the final comparison (§4.6). We re-implement these methods based on their respective public code.[10] We use the huggingface transformers library (Wolf et al., 2020) for our implementation. Complete setup details can be found in Appendix A.

**Evaluation:** We report ROUGE 1/2/L scores (R-1/2/L, Lin (2004)) on the XSum test set, BLEU scores (Papineni et al., 2002) on the en-ro test set, and accuracy on the MNLI and SST2 dev set. For MNLI and SST2, we take the median of five random runs. We also report the number of tuned parameters relative to that in full fine-tuning (#params).

## 4.2 THE RESULTS OF EXISTING METHODS

We first overview the results of existing methods on the four tasks. As shown in Figure 4 and Table 2, while existing methods can achieve competitive performance on MNLI and SST2 by tuning fewer than 1% parameters, a large gap is still present if we add 5% parameters in XSum and en-ro. The gap remains significant even though we increase the relative parameter size to _>_ 10%. Even larger gaps have been observed in Raffel et al. (2020) on high-resource MT tasks. This shows that many methods that claimed comparable results to full fine-tuning on the GLUE benchmark with an encoder-only model (Guo et al., 2021; Ben Zaken et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021), or on relatively simple generation benchmarks such as E2E (Novikova et al., 2017) with an encoder-decoder model (Li & Liang, 2021), may not generalize well to other standard benchmarks. The influencing factors could be complicated including the number of training samples, task complexity, or model architecture. We thus advocate for future research on this line to report results on more diverse benchmarks to exhibit a more complete picture of their performance profile. Below, our analysis will mainly focus on the XSum and en-ro datasets to better distinguish different design choices. We note that these two benchmarks are relatively high-resource performed with an encoder-decoder model (BART), while we will discuss the results on MNLI and SST2 with an encoder-only model (RoBERTa) in §4.6.

## 4.4 WHICH MODIFIED REPRESENTATION – ATTENTION OR FFN?

**Setup:** We now study the effect of modifying different representations. We mainly compare attention and FFN modification. For easier analysis we categorize methods that modifies any hidden representations in the attention sub-layer (e.g. the head output, query, etc) as modifying the attention module. We compare parallel adapters at attention and FFN and prefix tuning. We also transfer the FFN modification to LoRA to have a LoRA (ffn) variant for a complete comparison. Specifically, we use LoRA to approximate the parameter updates for the FFN weights _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][×][d][m]_ and _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[d][m][×][d]_ . In this case _**W**_ up in LoRA for _**W**_ 1 (similar for _**W**_ down of _**W**_ 2) would have dimensions of _r × dm_ , where _dm_ = 4 _d_ as described in §2.1. Thus we typically use smaller _r_ for LoRA (ffn) than other methods to match their overall parameter size in later experiments.

**Results:** As shown in Figure 5, _any_ method with FFN modification outperforms _all_ the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params), often with fewer parameters. Second, the same method applied at FFN always improves over its attention counterpart. For example, LoRA (ffn) improves LoRA (attn) by 1 R-2 points on XSum. We also highlight that prefix tuning does not keep improving when we further increase the capacity, which is also observed in Li & Liang (2021). These results suggest that _FFN modification can utilize the added parameters more effectively than attention, no matter what the functional form or composition function is_ . We hypothesize that this is because the FFN learns task-specific textual patterns (Geva et al., 2021), while attention learns pairwise positional interactions which do not require large capacity for adapting to new tasks.

**Is the story different when we use** 0 _._ 1% **parameters?** In §3.1 we reason that prefix tuning is more expressive than adapters (attn), which, however, is not reflected in Figure 5. We conjecture that this is because multi-head attention is only superior when the parameter budget is small. To validate this hypothesis, we compare prefix tuning to parallel adapters when they add 0 _._ 1% of the pretrained parameters. To ablate the impact of the composition function, we also report the results of removing the gating in prefix tuning as _**h**_ + ∆ _**h**_ . We include the results of the multi-head parallel adapter variant (MH PA) described in §3.3. As shown in Table 4, the multi-head methods – prefix tuning and MH PA (attn) – outperform all others by at least 1.6 BLEU points when using 0.1% of the parameters. Surprisingly, reducing _l_ from 200 to 30 only causes 0.4 BLEU loss for prefix tuning while PA (attn) loses 1.9 points. The gating composition function in prefix tuning slightly helps the results by 0.3 points. We highlight that the MH parallel adapter improves the single-headed version by 1.6 points, which again verifies the effectiveness of the multi-head formulation.

Combining the results in Figure 5 and Table 4, we conclude that _modifying head attention shows the best results when the parameter budget is very small, while the FFN can better utilize modifications at larger capacities._ This suggests that it may be effective to allocate a larger parameter budget to FFN modification instead of treating attention and FFN equally as in Houlsby et al. (2019).

## 4.5 WHICH COMPOSITION FUNCTION?

## 4.6 AN EFFECTIVE INTEGRATION BY TRANSFERRING FAVORABLE DESIGN ELEMENTS

We first highlight three findings in previous sections: (1) Scaled parallel adapter is the best variant to modify FFN; (2) FFN can better utilize modification at larger capacities; and (3) modifying head attentions like prefix tuning can achieve strong performance with only 0.1% parameters. Inspired by them, we mix and match the favorable designs behind these findings: specifically, we use prefix tuning with a small bottleneck dimension ( _l_ = 30) at the attention sub-layers and allocate more parameter budgets to modify FFN representation using the scaled parallel adapter ( _r_ = 512). Since prefix tuning can be viewed as a form of adapter in our unified framework, we name this variant as _Mix-And-Match adapter (MAM Adapter)_ . In Table 6, we compare MAM adapter with various parameter-efficient tuning methods. For completeness, we also present results of other combination versions in Table 6: using parallel adapters at both attention and FFN layers and combining prefix tuning (attn) with LoRA (ffn) – both of these combined versions can improve over their respective prototypes. However, MAM Adapter achieves the best performance on both tasks and is able to match the results of our full fine-tuning by only updating 6 _._ 7% of the pre-trained parameters. In Table 2, we present the results of MAM Adapter on MNLI and SST2 as well, where MAM Adapter achieves comparable results to full fine-tuning by adding only 0.5% of pretrained parameters.

## 5 DISCUSSION

We provide a unified framework for several performant parameter-tuning methods, which enables us to instantiate a more effective model that matches the performance of full fine-tuning method through transferring techniques across approaches. We hope our work can provide insights and guidance for future research on parameter-efficient tuning.

## ETHICS STATEMENT

Our work proposes a method for efficient fine-tuning of pre-trained models, in particular language models. Pre-trained language models have a wide variety of positive applications, such as the applications to summarization, translation, or language understanding described in our paper. At the same time, there are a number of ethical concerns with language models in general, including concerns regarding the generation of biased or discriminative text (Bordia & Bowman, 2019), the leakage of private information from training data (Carlini et al., 2020), and environmental impact of training or tuning them (Strubell et al., 2019).

Our method attempts to train language models making minimal changes to their pre-existing parameters. While it is an interesting research question whether parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods exacerbate, mitigate, or make little change to issues such as bias or information leakage, to our knowledge no previous work has examined this topic. It is an interesting avenue for future work.

With respect to environmental impact, the methods proposed in this paper add a small number of extra parameters and components to existing models, and thus they have a nominal negative impact on training and inference time – for example, the final MAM Adapter needs 100% - 150% training time of full fine-tuning in our four benchmarks since parameter-efficient tuning typically needs more epochs to converge; the inference time is roughly the same as the model obtained by full fine-tuning. On the other hand, as the methods proposed in this paper may obviate the need for full fine-tuning, this may also significantly reduce the cost (in terms of memory/deployed servers) of serving models. Notably, the great majority of the experimentation done for this paper was performed on a data center powered entirely by renewable energy.

## REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

In addition to the setup description in §4.1, we have detailed the complete experiments setup such as batch size, optimizer, learning rates in Appendix A. Besides, we have publicized our source code. These resources should be sufficient to reproduce results of the paper.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a timely and valuable contribution to the growing body of research on parameter-efficient transfer learning in NLP, offering a well-structured and insightful effort to unify disparate methods under a single conceptual framework. The motivation is strong and clearly articulated: as large language models become increasingly unwieldy to fine-tune in their entirety, lightweight alternatives are essential—but their design principles remain fragmented and poorly understood. The authors make a compelling case for a systematic decomposition of these methods by reframing them as mechanisms that modify hidden states in frozen pretrained models, which provides a coherent and generalizable perspective. This unified view enables not only deeper analysis of existing methods—such as adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA—but also the cross-pollination of design elements to create novel, more efficient variants. The empirical evaluation is thorough, spanning diverse tasks and domains, and the results are impressive, demonstrating that their proposed approach can match full fine-tuning performance while tuning fewer parameters. However, the clarity of the technical exposition suffers somewhat in places; the transition into the unified framework (§3.2) could benefit from more precise formalization, and the figure captions—particularly Figure 1—assume familiarity that may not be universal. Additionally, while the ablation studies support their claims, a more in-depth analysis of *why* certain design choices (e.g., multi-head adaptation) lead to better performance would strengthen the paper’s explanatory power. Overall, the paper is of high quality and makes a significant conceptual and practical advance, but would benefit from improved presentation precision and deeper mechanistic insights to fully realize its potential impact.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'modified representation' for prefix tuning within unified framework
comments.quote: "Modified Representation" indicates which hidden representation is directly modified." For example, ... prefix tuning uses ... _**x**_ , the input of the PLM layer, to compute ∆ _**h**_ , while adapters use _**h**_ , the output ...
comments.explanation: Prefix tuning modifies the key and value inputs to attention by concatenating _**P**_k and _**P**_v, not the output _**h**_. While algebraically plausible under certain assumptions, it conflates modifying input states (K/V) with modifying output states (h), which differ in implementation, gradient flow, and architectural intent. comments.title: Underspecification of multi-head parallel adapter architecture
comments.quote: Multi-head Parallel Adapter is a further step to make adapters more similar to prefix tuning: we apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs as prefix tuning.
comments.explanation: The proposal lacks critical implementation details. It does not specify whether adapter weights (W_down, W_up) are shared across heads or learned independently. Shared weights reduce capacity; independent weights increase parameter count by Nh, invalidating comparisons with standard adapters. Table 1 implies no increase, suggesting weight sharing, but this contradicts the motivation of mimicking prefix tuning’s per-head expressiveness. Additionally, it is unclear whether modification occurs before or after head concatenation, and whether layer normalization is applied per head or after. These choices significantly affect performance and parameter efficiency. Since the method is evaluated empirically, the lack of architectural specification harms reproducibility and weakens claims about its superiority over standard parallel adapters.

The paper does not clarify whether the FFN variant uses the initial layer input or the immediate sublayer input, blurring the distinction between insertion forms. comments.title: Lack of principled scaling factor (s) selection for LoRA and Scaled PA

Given the confidence intervals and variability in full fine-tuning performance (noted in footnote), 'comparable' is contextually acceptable but overreaches as a general claim. On classification tasks, it consistently trails. The statement implies parity across all tasks, which is not fully supported. More precise language (e.g., 'approaches' or 'matches on some tasks') would better reflect the results.

comments.title: Ambiguous training and environmental impact claims

Additionally, while renewable energy use is positive, it does not equate to lower environmental impact without reporting total energy or CO2. The statement may mislead readers into thinking the method is greener, when it might consume more energy. These claims are not technically incorrect but are underspecified and could be misinterpreted without clarification on time components and lack of compute accounting.

comments.title: Ambiguity in dimensionality of key and value matrices in prefix tuning
comments.quote:
_**P** k, _**P** v ∈_ R _[l][×][d]_ are concatenated with the original key _**K**_ and value _**V**_ . Then multi-head attention is performed on the new prefixed keys and values. The computation of head _i_ in Eq. 2 becomes:

_**P** k_ and _**P** v_ are split into _Nh_ head vectors respectively and _**P** k_[(] _[i]_[)] _[,]_ _**[ P]**_[ (] _v[i]_[)] _∈_ R _[l][×][d/N][h]_
comments.explanation:
At first glance, there appears to be a conflict in how the prefix matrices are split across heads. The text states that _**P** k and _**P** v ∈_ R^[l×d] are split into Nh heads, yielding _**P** k[i] and _**P** v[i] ∈ R^[l×d/Nh]. This suggests a partition of the d-dimensional space across Nh heads — which is standard in multi-head attention. However, R^[l×d] being split into Nh tensors of shape R^[l×d/Nh] implies that each head gets a d/Nh-dimensional slice of the value space, which is correct, but the notation _**P** k[i] ∈ R^[l×d/Nh] could be misinterpreted as if each head operates on a separate set of l prefix vectors projected into a lower-dimensional space. Crucially, this would incorrectly suggest that the total number of prefix vectors is l × Nh, which contradicts the intended design where only l shared prefix vectors are used across all heads.

However, upon closer inspection and in context — particularly Figure 1, the description of ‘splitting’ and the low-rank interpretation in §3.1 — it becomes clear that the l prefix vectors are shared across heads, and each head applies its own projection to them (via W_q, W_k, W_v in standard attention). The shape R^[l×d/Nh] is correct if interpreted as each head receiving a projected version of the l×d matrix into the head’s d/Nh-dimensional attention space, not as l distinct vectors per head. Thus, while mathematically defensible, the phrasing risks misleading readers into thinking the prefix vectors are head-specific, which they are not.

Standard multi-head treatment projects the same input across different heads; here, the same prefix vectors are shared, and projection is applied per head. The issue is not with the math but with how the split is described. A clearer way would be to say that _**P** k and _**P** v are broadcast across heads and then projected head-wise, rather than ‘split’.

comments.title: Inconsistent functional form of LoRA across equations and unified framework
comments.quote: For a specific input _**x**_ to the linear projection in multi-head attention, LoRA modifies the projection output _**h**_ as: _**h** ← _**xW** q + _**xW**_down_**W**_up_
comments.explanation:
In §2.2, the paper defines LoRA’s update as _**h** ← _**xW** q + _**xW**_down_**W**_up. This is inconsistent with the standard LoRA formulation where the update is additive to the weight, not to the output. That is, LoRA learns ∆_W_ = _W_↓_W_↑ and applies it as (_W_ + ∆_W_)_x_ = _Wx_ + _W_↓_W_↑_x_, which results in _**h** ← _**xW** q + _**xW**_down_**W**_up — so algebraically it is correct. However, the expression gives the impression that the low-rank update is being added to the output, rather than being part of the weight update. While this is mathematically equivalent, it obscures the mechanism and deviates from how LoRA is typically presented (as a weight decomposition).

Later in Table 1, the functional form for LoRA is listed as _**xW**_down_**W**_up_, which excludes the base _**xW** q term — consistent with ∆_**h**_ = _**xW**_down_**W**_up. This aligns with the unified framework’s abstraction where ∆_**h**_ is the modification added to _**h**_. So while the decomposition in Table 1 is consistent with the framework, the presentation in the text could mislead readers into thinking LoRA directly modifies the output functionally like adapters, rather than indirectly via weight update. The confusion arises from conflating implementation-level output rewriting with modular abstraction.

Still, given that the authors clarify in the unified framework that LoRA uses _**h** ← _**h** + _s_·∆_**h**_ and define ∆_**h**_ = _**xW**_down_**W**_up, the framework resolves the issue. But the initial presentation in §2.2 is slightly misleading without emphasizing that this rewriting assumes linearity of the transformation.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of ‘modified representation’ for prefix tuning
comments.quote: "Modified Representation" indicates which hidden representation is directly modified." For example, ... prefix tuning uses ... _**x**_ , the input of the PLM layer, to compute ∆ _**h**_ , while adapters use _**h**_ , the output ...
comments.explanation:
The text claims that prefix tuning computes ∆_**h**_ from _**x**_, the input to the layer, and applies it to the attention head output, which matches the unified view. However, the modified representation is listed in Table 1 as ‘head attn’ for prefix tuning. This raises a potential ambiguity: is ∆_**h**_ added to the head output before concatenation (as head-wise residual), or is it modifying the K and V matrices?

From §2.2, prefix tuning prepends _**P** k and _**P** v to _**K** and _**V**, which means it modifies the key and value inputs to the attention function, not the output _**h**_. Thus, strictly speaking, the ‘modified representation’ is not the attention output _**h**_, but the cached key/value states used in attention computation. This contradicts the unified framework’s framing of all methods as adding ∆_**h**_ to a hidden state.

However, the authors justify this abstraction in §3.1 by re-framing prefix tuning as producing a ∆_**h**_ = softmax(_**xW**_q _**P**_k^T) _**P**_v, which is then additively combined (via gating) with the original head output. This is an *alternative derivation*, not the original implementation. While algebraically valid under certain structural assumptions, it equates two conceptually different operations: modifying input features (K/V) vs. modifying output features (h).

The framework is useful, but the conflation risks misleading readers into thinking prefix tuning *operationally* adds a delta to the output, when it actually modifies the internal attention inputs. This distinction matters for implementation and gradient flow. The authors note this is an ‘alternative form’, so it's not an outright error, but the unified view slightly over-abstracts the mechanism.

comments.title: Missing scaling factor in softmax for prefix tuning derivation
comments.quote: Without loss of generalization, we ignore the softmax scaling factor ~~_√_~~ _d_ for ease of notation.
comments.explanation:
The paper explicitly acknowledges omitting the 1/√_d_k factor in the softmax computation (Eq. 1 and surrounding discussion). This is standard in many presentations for notational simplicity, and the footnote justifies it as not affecting generalization. In practice, this scaling is always included in implementations, and since the paper's focus is on architectural comparison rather than gradient dynamics, omitting it is acceptable.

However, later in §3.1, when deriving the functional similarity between prefix tuning and adapters, the paper writes:

∆_**h**_ = softmax(_**xW**_q _**P**_k^T) _**P**_v

This expression lacks the scaling, and no indication is given that it's omitted for clarity. Since this equation is used to draw analogies to adapter functionality, and softmax outputs are sensitive to input scale, the absence of scaling could affect the interpretation of how ‘well-behaved’ the gating weights λ are.

Still, given the footnote explicitly stating the omission and the fact that all models would apply the same scaling in practice, this does not constitute a technical error. The derivation remains valid under consistent omission. Therefore, while a careful reader might briefly wonder about scale mismatch, context resolves it.

comments.title: Incorrect parameter count implication in multi-head expressiveness claim
comments.quote: Notably, prefix tuning is not adding more parameters than adapters when _l_ = _r_ .[7] We empirically validate such multi-head influence in §4.4.
comments.explanation:
The claim is that when l = r, prefix tuning does not add more parameters than adapters. This is true in isolation: _P_k, P_v ∈ R^[l×d] gives 2×l×d parameters per layer, while an adapter has W_down ∈ R^[d×r] and W_up ∈ R^[r×d], totaling 2×d×r. So when l = r, both have ~2×d×r parameters.

However, the prior sentence states: ‘we have full rank updates to each attention head if l ≥ d/Nh, but we only get full-rank updates to the whole attention output with adapters if r ≥ d’. This implies that prefix tuning achieves per-head full rank with l = d/Nh, requiring only ~2×d×(d/Nh) parameters, while adapters need r = d (i.e. ~2×d×d) for full rank on the entire output.

So yes, with l = d/Nh (≪ d), prefix tuning can achieve high expressiveness per head without matching r = d in adapters. But the claim ‘prefix tuning is not adding more parameters than adapters when l = r’ compares equality, not the regime where l ≪ r yet performance differs. The issue is not falsehood, but framing: it downplays the fact that prefix tuning can be much more parameter-efficient *for the same expressiveness per head*.

The comparison is fair in terms of raw parameter count when l = r, but the context of the surrounding argument — about expressiveness — makes this seem like a neutral comparison when actually prefix tuning has a representational advantage per parameter. The claim is technically correct but potentially misleading in this context. It should clarify that while parameter counts are similar when l = r, the *distribution* of those parameters across heads gives prefix tuning greater head-wise flexibility.

comments.title: Under-specified composition function for multi-head parallel adapter
comments.quote: Multi-head Parallel Adapter is a further step to make adapters more similar to prefix tuning: we apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs as prefix tuning.
comments.explanation:
The paper proposes ‘multi-head parallel adapter’ by applying a parallel adapter to each attention head’s output. However, it does not specify how this is done. Is there a separate adapter per head? Or is the same adapter applied to each head? If separate, the parameter count increases by Nh; if shared, it reduces capacity.

From Table 1, the proposed variant has the same functional form as standard adapter: ReLU(_**hW**_down)_**W**_up, and the ‘modified representation’ is ‘head attn’. But this still doesn’t resolve whether the adapter weights are shared across heads or not. Given that prefix tuning shares _P_k and _P_v across sequences but allows per-head projection, a natural parallel would be to share W_down and W_up across heads. But that’s not stated.

Moreover, the functional form assumes _**h**_ is the head output (dimension d/Nh), so W_down ∈ R^[(d/Nh)×r], W_up ∈ R^[r×(d/Nh)]. But then concatenation happens after modification — how are the modified head outputs combined? The paper says nothing about this, nor whether layer norm is applied per head or after concatenation.

Since the method is evaluated in §4.4, it must have been implemented, but the description lacks key architectural choices. This rises above minor vagueness because the performance could vary significantly based on weight sharing and normalization placement. The comparison to prefix tuning also depends on fair parameter matching, which cannot be verified without knowing the structure.

comments.title: Mismatch between attention input dimensions and model dimension in MHA equation
comments.quote:
Given a sequence of _m_ vectors _**C** ∈_ R _[m][×][d]_ over which we would like to perform attention and a query vector _**x** ∈_ R _[d]_ , multi-head attention (MHA) computes the output on each head and concatenates them:[3]

MHA( _**C** ,_ _**x**_ ) = Concat(head1 _, · · · ,_ headh) _**W** o,_ headi = Attn( _**xW** q_[(] _[i]_[)] _[,]_ _**[ CW]** k_[ (] _[i]_[)] _[,]_ _**[ CW]** v_[ (] _[i]_[)][)] _[,]_ (2)
comments.explanation: At first glance, this equation appears problematic because it uses a single query vector _**x** ∈_ R^[d]^ as input to the attention function, while attention typically operates on a sequence of queries. The standard multi-head attention mechanism in Transformers takes a sequence of queries (e.g., from the previous layer), not a single vector. This raises concern that the formulation may be oversimplified or incorrect. However, the footnote [3] states: 'Below, we sometimes ignore the head index _i_ to simplify notation when there is no confusion.' This suggests that this is a notational convenience. Moreover, the input context _**C** ∈_ R^[m×d]^ is properly defined as a sequence, but _**x**_ is defined as a single vector, which breaks symmetry. In real models, queries are also a matrix _**Q** ∈_ R^[n×d]^, not a vector. This equation only makes sense if all queries are identical copies of _**x**_ — an implausible assumption. While the core idea of projecting inputs via _W_q, _W_k, _W_v_ is correct, the formalism here incorrectly reduces the query side to a singleton. Although this may be a simplification for illustrative purposes, it contradicts the standard Transformer formulation presented initially in the paper (Section 2.1 mentions queries _**Q** ∈_ R^[n×d_k]^), and no clarification is given for why it's being reduced to one query. This could mislead a reader about how attention is parameterized in real PLMs. It's particularly concerning because the subsequent analysis (e.g., on prefix tuning) depends on how _**x**_ is used to compute modifications. The mismatch persists even assuming OCR fixes (no symbol confusion).

comments.title: Ambiguity in LoRA's modification of projection output in text vs. Table 1
comments.quote: For a specific input _**x**_ to the linear projection in multi-head attention, LoRA modifies the projection output _**h**_ as:
comments.explanation: The sentence sets up an expectation that an equation defining how LoRA modifies _**h**_ will follow immediately. However, no equation appears after this statement in the provided passage. The reader expects something like _**h** ← _**x**(_**W** + _**W**_down_**W**_up)_ or similar, especially since earlier methods (adapters, prefix tuning) were defined with explicit update rules. Instead, the passage jumps to a discussion of Figure 3 and the similarity to adapters. While Table 1 later provides the functional form for LoRA as **_xW_**down**_W_**up, this is defined as ∆**_h_**, and it's not explicitly composed with the original output. The text suggests a concrete operation is about to be defined, but fails to deliver it. A careful reader might infer from context that LoRA modifies the weight matrix and thus indirectly changes _**h** = _**xW**_, but the phrasing 'modifies the projection output _**h**_' implies a direct modification of the output, aligning more with the unified framework’s ∆**_h_** view. This creates tension: is LoRA updating weights (implicit) or outputs (explicit)? The unified framework in Section 3.2 reconciles this by interpreting LoRA as producing ∆**_h** = _**xW**_down_**W**_up, added to the original _**h**_ via scaling (with _s_), but this conceptual shift isn't clearly bridged in the 2.2 overview. The explanation in 2.2 remains underspecified and could mislead a reader into thinking LoRA operates directly on activations rather than weights, especially without the immediate equation.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of _dm_ vs. _d_m in FFN dimension notation
comments.quote: where _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][×][d][m]_ , _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[d][m][×][d]_ . Transformers typically use a large _dm_ , e.g. _dm_ = 4 _d_ .
comments.explanation: The notation switches between _d_m (in subscripts for matrix dimensions) and _dm_ (in prose), which could suggest a typo or OCR error. For example, R^[d×d_m]^ is standard, but the text shows R^[d][×][d][m], which likely arose from failed OCR parsing of subscripts. However, the real issue is in the prose: 'e.g. _dm_ = 4 _d_'. The variable _dm_ appears to be intended as _d_m (the hidden dimension of the FFN). In the field, it's conventional to denote the intermediate FFN dimension as _d_ff or _d_inner or _d_model×4, not _dm_. The use of _dm_ is ambiguous—it could be misread as a product of _d_ and _m_, which would be dimensionally incorrect. While the surrounding context (e.g., Table 1's use of _d_) confirms _d_ is the model dimension, and the value '4d' is standard, the notation _dm_ is nonstandard and potentially misleading. Given that OCR likely mangled _d_m into _dm_, and the equation format suggests failed subscript parsing (e.g., [d][×][d][m]), this is best interpreted as a notation inconsistency due to OCR, not a conceptual error. Still, even under the most plausible fix (_d_m_), the prose _dm_ = 4_d_ should be _d_m = 4d_ for consistency. This is a minor issue because experts would parse it correctly, but it could confuse less experienced readers.

comments.title: Unclear derivation of prefix tuning functional form in Table 1
comments.quote:
|Prefx Tuning|softmax(**_xW_**_q_**_P_** _⊤_
k_ )**_P_**_v_|parallel|head attn|**_h_**_←_(1_−λ_)**_h_**+_λ_∆**_h_**|
comments.explanation: The functional form listed for prefix tuning—softmax(_**xW_qP_k**^⊤__)_**P_v**—does not match the standard computation in prefix tuning. In prefix tuning, the modification comes from prepending prefix vectors to the key and value sequences, not from applying a softmax-weighted combination of _**P_v** based on _**xW_q** and _**P_k**. That form looks more like a form of soft attention or retrieval, not the concatenation-based mechanism described in the text. The actual mechanism is: new_K = Concat(_**P_k**, _**C W_k**_), new_V = Concat(_**P_v**, _**C W_v**_), then compute attention as usual. The resulting head output is not simply a function of the form shown. The only way this form could be valid is if prefix tuning were equivalent to adding a learned residual component ∆_**h**_ proportional to an attention over the prefix vectors, which might be an interpretation in the limit, but it is not the actual computation. The text in Section 3.1 hints at a 'weighted addition', and footnote [6] says prefix tuning performs 'weighted addition', but this is still a mischaracterization. The listed functional form in Table 1 appears to conflate prefix tuning with a different mechanism (e.g., retrieval-augmented attention). This is concerning because the entire unified framework rests on accurately representing each method’s ∆_**h**_. While the composition rule (gated addition) might be approximated in some way, the functional form is not a correct decomposition. The authors may be attempting to express the influence of the prefix on the attention output, but doing so in this equation form misrepresents the computation. This undermines the validity of the unified framework’s decomposition, at least for prefix tuning. The issue is not resolved by context—it persists even after reading the description in 3.1 and the footnote [6].

comments.title: Inconsistency in prefix tuning description: applies to 'keys and values of multi-head attention', but Table 1 says 'head attn'
comments.quote:
prefix tuning prepends _l_ tunable prefix vectors to the keys and values of the multihead attention at every layer.

|Prefx Tuning|...|parallel|head attn|...
comments.explanation: The text says prefix tuning modifies the keys and values of the multi-head attention, which operates at the level of the full attention input (before head splitting), while Table 1 lists the modified representation as 'head attn'—suggesting it modifies the output of each attention head. These are different representations. The actual mechanism, as described, prepends prefixes to _**K**_ and _**V**_ before multi-head processing. After projection via _**W_k[i]**, _**P_k** is split into per-head components _**P_k[i]**_ ∈_ R^[l×d/Nh]^, so the modification is applied at the head level during attention computation. However, the representation being modified is not the output _head_i_, but rather the input _**K, V**_ to the attention function. The result is that the final head outputs are affected, but the direct modification is not to _head_i_ itself. Table 1's 'head attn' as modified representation is ambiguous: it could mean the head-level attention computation (plausible), but more likely misleads the reader into thinking the method operates on the output of each head, like an adapter on head outputs. This contrasts with adapter, which modifies ffn/attn outputs (i.e., post-sublayer). The term 'head attn' is underspecified. While the authors might intend it to mean 'attention at the head level', the label differs from the others (ffn/attn, attn key/val) and could be interpreted as modifying the head output representation _after_ composition. This creates ambiguity in how the 'modified representation' dimension is defined. Given that prefix tuning modifies inputs to the attention function (keys and values) rather than the output, 'attn key/val' might be more accurate, aligning with LoRA. The inconsistency weakens the clarity of the unifying framework.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'full-rank updates' claim for prefix tuning vs. adapters
comments.quote: basically we have full rank updates to each attention head if $ l \geq d/Nh $, but we only get full-rank updates to the whole attention output with adapters if $ r \geq d $.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement seems to suggest that prefix tuning is more expressive due to multi-head decomposition, which aligns with the paper's broader point about design differences. The authors are correct that each head in prefix tuning operates in a space of dimension $ d_h = d / Nh $, and if $ l \geq d_h $, the prefix can in principle span the full space for that head. However, the phrase 'full-rank updates' is ambiguous. For a modification $ \Delta h $ to achieve full-rank update capability across the entire attention output (dimension $ d $), adapters would indeed need $ r \geq d $, as their bottleneck limits the rank of $ \Delta h $. In contrast, prefix tuning applies a shared low-rank update per head, but across $ Nh $ heads, the total number of tunable parameters in prefix matrices $ P_k, P_v \in \mathbb{R}^{l \times d} $ is $ 2 \cdot l \cdot d $ — not per head — so even if $ l \geq d/Nh $, the rank per head is limited by $ l $, and the combined update across heads is still rank-limited globally. The claim could mislead a reader into thinking that $ l \geq d/Nh $ gives full-rank expressivity over the full $ d $-dimensional space, which is incorrect. The key clarification missing is that prefix tuning enables *per-head* full-rank expressivity (if $ l \geq d_h $) with fewer total parameters than adapters needing $ r \geq d $ for global full-rank — a valid and important point, but not clearly stated. The current wording risks conflating per-head and global rank expressivity.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of $ \Delta h $ in functional form description
comments.quote: The functional forms of all these methods are similar with a proj ~~d~~ own _→_ nonlinear _→_ proj ~~u~~ p architecture, while “nonlinear” degenerates to the identity function in LoRA.
comments.explanation: The text refers to $ \Delta h $ being computed via a 'project-down → nonlinear → project-up' pattern in adapters and prefix tuning, which matches Equation 4 and the paper's earlier framing. For adapters, $ \Delta h = \text{ReLU}(h W_\text{down}) W_\text{up} $, which fits perfectly. For prefix tuning, however, $ \Delta h $ is defined as $ \text{softmax}(x W_q P_k^\top) P_v $, which is qualitatively different: it involves a softmax over computed attention weights and does not decompose as a single-layer projection followed by nonlinearity and another projection in the same way. While one could squint and see $ P_k^\top $ as 'down-projection', $ \text{softmax}(\cdot) $ as nonlinearity, and $ P_v $ as 'up-projection', this is a stretch — the intermediate value is not a bottleneck representation but attention weights of size $ n \times l $ (sequence length times prefix length), and the output depends on both input and global prefix context. In contrast, LoRA’s $ \Delta h = x W_\text{down} W_\text{up} $ clearly fits the 'linear → identity → linear' form. Thus, the claim that prefix tuning shares the same functional form architecture is misleading; it shares a low-rank matrix decomposition intuition but not the same computational graph. This could mislead readers about the structural similarity between adapters and prefix tuning. The unified framework still holds, but the functional form is not as uniformly shared as claimed.

comments.title: Misleading comparison of insertion form in unified framework
comments.quote: traditionally adapters are inserted at a position in a sequential manner, where both the input and output are $ \mathbf{h} $. Prefix tuning and LoRA – although not originally described in this way – turn out to be equivalent to a parallel insertion where $ \mathbf{x} $ is the input.
comments.explanation: The authors are correct in intent: adapters typically sit in the residual stream after a sublayer, so $ h_\text{out} = h + f(h) $, making $ f $ sequential (dependent on $ h $). In contrast, prefix tuning introduces $ P_k, P_v $ that are independent of the current input $ x $, and are prepended to keys and values — so the computation path is parallel. Similarly, LoRA applies a low-rank update to a weight matrix, which modifies the transformation from $ x $ to $ h $, so it’s effectively in parallel with the original path. So the high-level idea of 'parallel vs. sequential' is sound and insightful. However, the phrasing 'where both the input and output are $ h $' for sequential insertion is confusing, because in reality the adapter takes $ h $ as input and outputs $ \Delta h $, which is added to $ h $. The full module input is $ h $, output is $ h + \Delta h $, not $ h $. In contrast, for parallel insertion (e.g. LoRA), the input is $ x $, and output is a modified $ h = x (W + \Delta W) $. So while the classification is justified, the description of 'input and output' as both being $ h $ for adapters is inaccurate and could confuse a reader about the data flow. A better phrasing would be that the adapter modifies the output of the module in a post-hoc, additive way (sequential in execution), while LoRA and prefix tuning modify the computation path from $ x $ to $ h $ (parallel).

comments.title: Unclear definition of 'direct input $ x $' in unified framework
comments.quote: we denote the hidden representation to be directly modified as $ \mathbf{h} $, and the direct input to the PLM sub-module that computes $ \mathbf{h} $ as $ \mathbf{x} $ (e.g. $ \mathbf{h} $ and $ \mathbf{x} $ can be the attention output and input respectively).
comments.explanation: This definition seems clear at first: $ x $ is the input to the sublayer that produces $ h $. For attention, $ x $ would be the query vector. But in prefix tuning, $ \Delta h $ is computed using $ x W_q $, not $ x $ itself — and $ P_k, P_v $ are fixed per layer. So the function computing $ \Delta h $ depends on $ x $ only via $ W_q $, but $ W_q $ is frozen. So strictly speaking, the prefix tuning module does not take $ x $ as input unless we include the frozen projection. The framework assumes that $ \Delta h $ is computed from $ x $ via a learned function, but in prefix tuning, $ \Delta h $ is not directly a function of $ x $ — it’s a function of $ x W_q $, and the prefix matrices $ P_k, P_v $ are tuned, not the mapping from $ x $. Also, in LoRA, $ \Delta h = x \Delta W $, which is linear in $ x $, so $ x $ is a direct input. But in adapters, $ \Delta h = f(h) $, which depends on $ h $, not $ x $. So the framework’s clean separation hinges on this $ x $ vs $ h $ distinction, but the way $ x $ is used varies across methods — sometimes it’s transformed by frozen weights, sometimes not. While this doesn’t break the framework, it leaves the definition of 'direct input' slightly underspecified. A careful reader might ask: is $ x $ pre- or post-projection? The paper doesn’t clarify, which could matter for reproducibility in edge cases.

comments.title: Inconsistent representation of bottleneck dimension in parameter count comparison between prefix tuning and adapters
comments.quote: prefix tuning uses the same number of parameters as adapters, while LoRA uses more parameters.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this statement appears consistent: prefix tuning uses 2 × l × d parameters (for P_k and P_v) and adapters use 2 × r × d (for W_down and W_up), so if l = r, they have the same parameter count. However, the key issue arises from Table 1 and the functional form analysis in §3.1: prefix tuning's functional form is given as softmax(xW_qP_k^T)P_v. This expression implicitly requires a projection from x to weights, meaning that additional trainable parameters W_qP_k are involved — specifically, the matrix W_1 ∈ R^{d_h × l} mentioned earlier in the text is used to transform x into keys compatible with the prefix. If this W_1 is tunable (as is the case in Li & Liang, 2021), then the total number of parameters in prefix tuning should be 2 × l × d_h × N_h + l × d (for P_k and P_v) — unless W_1 is tied or fixed. However, the current text assumes that all parameter-efficient methods only tune the added modules and treats P_k and P_v as the only trainable parameters. The summary in §4.1 says prefix tuning uses '2 × l × d' parameters, which only matches the full count if the projections from x to the prefix space are not tunable. But in the standard implementation (and in Li & Liang, 2021), these projections are indeed trainable. Contextually, the authors seem to have forgotten to account for the additional l × d parameters from the W_q or equivalent projection, resulting in an underestimate of prefix tuning’s parameter count. This undermines fair comparison in tables like Table 3, where performance is compared at fixed '3.6%' or '0.1%' parameter budgets. If the true parameter count of prefix tuning is higher than stated, then the comparisons are misleading. While the paragraph attempts to clarify parameter counting, it oversimplifies and omits a critical component of the model's architecture.

comments.title: Ambiguous equivalence between LoRA (ffn) and other methods due to differing bottleneck scaling
comments.quote: Thus we typically use smaller _r_ for LoRA (ffn) than other methods to match their overall parameter size in later experiments.
comments.explanation: The paper rightly acknowledges that LoRA (ffn) has a much larger parameter footprint per bottleneck dimension r because the FFN weight matrices are size d × d_m with d_m = 4d. Specifically, W_down ∈ R^{4d × r} and W_up ∈ R^{r × d}, or vice versa, leading to O(r × d_m) = O(4 r d) parameters per layer. In contrast, LoRA in attention acts on W_q and W_v each of size d × d, so total LoRA parameters are 2 × r × d × 2 = 4 r d (across both matrices), similar per-layer cost. But when LoRA is applied to FFN, one must project into a higher-dimensional space — so even with the same r, LoRA (ffn) uses more parameters. The paper states it uses smaller r to compensate, which is correct in principle. However, the ambiguity lies in *how small* — if r is chosen such that 4 r_ffn d = 2 r_adapter d_m = 8 r_adapter d (since d_m = 4d), then r_ffn = 2 r_adapter. But if this scaling is not explicitly defined, the comparison of different r values across methods becomes underspecified. Worse, in Table 3 and Figure 5, results are plotted against fine-tuned parameters (%), but without stating how r maps to parameter count for LoRA (ffn), a reader cannot reproduce the exact settings. While the authors may internally correct for this, the lack of explicit calibration risks invalidating cross-method trends. For example, claiming 'LoRA (ffn) improves LoRA (attn) by 1 R-2 point' only holds if the parameter budget is fairly matched — but the budgeting strategy is not quantified.

comments.title: Potential overstatement of parallel adapter performance due to inconsistent reporting of bottleneck dimension
comments.explanation: In Table 3, the entry for PA (ffn), r=200 shows 2.4% tuned parameters and superior BLEU score of 36.4 on en-ro (vs 35.6 for SA (ffn) at same r). However, earlier in §4.1, the authors state that both adapter at attention and FFN use 2 × r × d parameters — which would mean that, for fixed r and shared d across layers, both should use the same relative parameter count. Yet PA (attn) is listed at 3.6% while PA (ffn) is at 2.4%, despite both using r=200. This discrepancy suggests that either: (a) the parameter counts are normalized layer-wise differently (e.g., only one FFN per layer vs. three attn types), or (b) there's inconsistency in how the percentage is computed. BART has 12 layers, and both FFN and attention occur once per layer — so unless adapter is only applied to a subset (e.g., only encoder or decoder), both should scale linearly with r. The mismatch suggests non-uniform deployment (e.g., more adapter layers for PA attn), but this is not stated. This raises concern about comparability: is PA (ffn) better because of its insertion form or because it's less parameter-heavy at same r, allowing better optimization? Without clarification, the claim that 'parallel adapter beats sequential adapters in all cases' may conflate architectural benefit with parameter efficiency per r. The authors correctly argue for framework-driven design but overstate empirical superiority without isolating variables.

comments.title: Multi-head parallel adapter description lacks specification of weight sharing across heads
comments.quote: Multi-head Parallel Adapter is a further step to make adapters more similar to prefix tuning: we apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs as prefix tuning.
comments.explanation: The concept of applying adapters per-head is intuitive and promising, as it mirrors the modularity of multi-head attention. However, the critical design choice — whether the adapter weights (W_down and W_up) are shared across heads or learned independently — is left completely unspecified. In prefix tuning, the prefixes P_k and P_v are split into head-specific components (each of size l × d/N_h), suggesting independent projection per head. If the multi-head parallel adapter uses head-specific W_down and W_up, the total parameter count becomes N_h times higher than a single adapter for the same r — making direct comparison invalid. Alternatively, if weights are shared across heads, the expressiveness drops significantly. The paper claims in §3.1 that 'prefix tuning is not adding more parameters than adapters when l = r', implying that l and r are directly comparable and that prefix parameters scale with l but not N_h — which only makes sense if the prefix vectors are projected head-individually but low-rank across the sequence. Yet for MH PA, no such clarification is given. Later in Table 1, MH PA is assigned the same functional form and parameter count pattern as PA, suggesting no increase with N_h — implying weight sharing. But this contradicts the motivation of mimicking prefix tuning’s per-head expressiveness. The method is therefore underspecified: a reader cannot implement it without making critical assumptions about parameterization. This harms reproducibility and weakens the empirical claims in Table 4 and Figure 5.

comments.title: Missing justification for FFN being more effective than attention for parameter-efficient tuning
comments.quote: We hypothesize that this is because the FFN learns task-specific textual patterns (Geva et al., 2021), while attention learns pairwise positional interactions which do not require large capacity for adapting to new tasks.
comments.explanation: The paper observes a consistent performance advantage for FFN-modifying methods over attention-modifying ones across tasks and methods — a valuable empirical insight. However, the stated hypothesis for *why* this occurs is underjustified. While Geva et al. (2021) show that FFNs encode task-specific knowledge such as factual associations, the claim that 'attention learns pairwise positional interactions' oversimplifies attention's role. Attention heads are known to capture syntactic, semantic, and even localized factual relations (Clark et al., 2019; Hergarten et al., 2020), not just position. Moreover, if attention were truly less important for downstream adaptation, then prefix tuning — which directly modifies attention — should underperform. Yet Figure 4 and Table 2 show it performs well, especially on text classification and MNLI. The hypothesis fails to reconcile this. More concretely, the benefit of FFN modification may stem not from intrinsic superiority in learning task patterns, but from architectural factors: FFNs have larger parameter counts (since d_m = 4d), making low-rank updates more effective; or, FFNs act as pointwise non-linear transformations, which may allow cleaner separation between frozen and tuned paths. Without deeper analysis — such as ablation by layer or probing the rank efficiency — the conclusion risks being post-hoc. Given that this is one of the central findings (§4.4), the paper misses an opportunity to provide stronger mechanistic explanation.

comments.title: Inconsistent bottleneck dimension scaling between LoRA (ffn) and comparable methods
comments.quote: We set _r_ as 512 for adapters and 102 for LoRA so that their tuned parameters are the same.
comments.explanation:
The text claims that r = 512 for adapters and r = 102 for LoRA (ffn) result in the same number of tuned parameters. However, based on the PAPER SUMMARY, for FFN modules, W1 ∈ ℝ^[d×dm] and W2 ∈ ℝ^[dm×d] with dm = 4d. For a parallel adapter modifying FFN, the parameters are 2 × r × d per module (via W_down ∈ ℝ^[d×r], W_up ∈ ℝ^[r×d]). For LoRA applied to FFN, it would typically apply to W1 or W2; if applied to W1 ∈ ℝ^[d×4d], LoRA uses r × d + r × 4d = 5 × r × d parameters (for W_down ∈ ℝ^[d×r], W_up ∈ ℝ^[r×4d]).

Thus, for the same r, LoRA (ffn) uses roughly 2.5× more parameters than an adapter. To match parameter counts, if adapter uses r_a = 512, then LoRA would need r_l such that 5 × r_l × d = 2 × 512 × d → r_l ≈ 205. But the paper uses r_l = 102, which would result in only about 510 × d parameters versus 1024 × d for the adapter—less than half. This contradicts the claim that the parameter budgets are matched.

The paper does state in the PAPER SUMMARY assumption: 'Smaller r is used for LoRA (ffn) to match parameter budget due to larger W_up dimension (r × dm)', which shows the authors are aware. However, using r = 102 vs r = 512 implies a ratio of ~5:2, not the required ~2.5:1 scaling. The mismatch suggests either a calculation error or underspecification. Even if r = 102 was chosen to approximate a budget, it falls significantly short unless only part of the FFN is modified.

This undermines the fairness of the comparison in Table 5, where LoRA (s=4) is said to outperform PA—unless the parameter counts are actually unequal, which would favor LoRA by giving it more capacity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'parallel adapter' insertion point and functional input
comments.quote: Parallel adapter (PA, ffn),_r_=1024
comments.explanation:
The term 'parallel adapter' is introduced in §3.3 as transferring the parallel insertion form of prefix tuning into adapters, meaning it uses the input x to the PLM module rather than the output h. However, when applied to the FFN module, it's unclear what 'x' refers to—specifically, is it the input to the entire transformer layer (before attention), or the input to the FFN sublayer (after the attention add & norm)?

In the case of FFN modification, if the parallel adapter uses the post-attention hidden state (i.e., the input to FFN), then it is effectively a sequential adapter in terms of information flow, even if structurally 'parallel'. The distinction between 'parallel' and 'sequential' hinges on whether the adapter depends on the output of the module it's modifying (sequential) or on the input (parallel). But for FFN, using its input x (which is h from attention) means the modification still depends on the full preceding computation, unlike prefix tuning which uses the original token input.

The unified framework in §3.2 defines 'x' as the direct input to the sub-module computing h. For FFN, that would be the post-attention output. So a 'parallel adapter' modifying FFN and using x would not be parallel in the same sense as prefix tuning modifying attention using the original input x. This blurs the claimed design distinction. The lack of clarity makes it hard to assess whether the performance gains are due to true parallel computation or simply architectural placement.

Context from Figure 3 and Table 1 supports that PA uses the same functional form as adapter but with parallel insertion, but without specifying what x is for FFN, reproduction is ambiguous.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency in prefix tuning parameter count
comments.quote: Prefx tuning (Li & Liang, 2021),_l_=200|3.6%
comments.explanation:
Table 6 states that prefix tuning with l=200 uses 3.6% of parameters. According to the PAPER SUMMARY, prefix tuning uses 2 × l × d parameters per layer (for P_k and P_v), and there are L layers. BART-large has 12 encoder and 12 decoder layers, so 24 total. Let d = 1024 (standard for BART-large). Then total parameters = 24 × 2 × 200 × 1024 = 9,830,400.

Full fine-tuning BART-large has about 406M parameters. 9,830,400 / 406M ≈ 2.42%, not 3.6%. Even if only applied to decoder (12 layers), it would be 1.21%. To reach 3.6%, l would need to be around l ≈ 300 (24×2×300×1024 ≈ 14.7M; 14.7M / 406M ≈ 3.6%).

The authors may be using a different model (e.g., mBART) or different d, but Table 6 is for XSum using BART-large. Alternatively, they might be including additional parameters (e.g., for gating), but the standard prefix tuning does not. The mismatch suggests either an error in l, d, or layer count assumptions, or a miscalculation in the percentage. This affects interpretation of parameter efficiency claims, especially when comparing to adapter or LoRA variants at different percentages.

comments.title: Missing explanation for scaling factor _s_ in LoRA and Scaled PA
comments.explanation:
The text evaluates LoRA and Scaled PA with s=4 but does not explain how this value was chosen. It mentions that 'We select _s_ based on the R-2 score on the dev set', but this risks overfitting to the dev set unless cross-validation is used. More importantly, the purpose of scaling in LoRA is not theoretically justified—unlike in the original LoRA paper, where it may relate to initialization or variance preservation.

In the original LoRA (Hu et al., 2021), the scaling is often s = α/r, where α is a constant, to maintain output magnitude after low-rank update. But here, α or such a rule is not mentioned. Without specifying how s is set (e.g., fixed, tuned, or derived), the method is underspecified. The claim that 'scaling helps' is empirical, but without knowing how s was selected across methods or whether it was optimized per method, the conclusion that 'scaling is better' may reflect hyperparameter tuning advantage rather than architectural superiority.

The footnote mentions that s can significantly affect results, reinforcing the need for clarity. The authors do the right thing by ablated s=1, but the lack of principled s-setting weakens reproducibility and generalization claims.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'head attention' modification in multi-head parallel adapter
comments.quote: MH PA (attn) — multi-head parallel adapter
comments.explanation:
The paper claims that MH PA applies parallel adapters per attention head, modifying 'head attention outputs'. However, it does not specify whether the adapter is applied to each head's output before concatenation (i.e., to vectors of size d/Nh), or after. This is critical: if applied before concatenation, the adapter would have W_down ∈ ℝ^[(d/Nh)×r] and W_up ∈ ℝ^[r×(d/Nh)], making it truly head-specific. If after, it would be on the full d-dimensional output.

The analogy to prefix tuning suggests head-level modification, as P_k^[i], P_v^[i] are per-head. But prefix tuning operates in the key/value space, not the output space. The functional form of the adapter (ReLU(h W_down) W_up) would require redefining h as the per-head output. Without clarification, it's unclear whether MH PA actually increases expressiveness or merely replicates parameters.

Moreover, if r is the same as in single-head PA, then total parameters would be Nh × 2 × r × (d/Nh) = 2 × r × d—same as single-head. But if r is total bottleneck, then per-head capacity drops. The paper doesn't specify how r is defined in MH PA, making replication impossible. This ambiguity undermines the claim that MH PA improves over PA by 1.6 BLEU points 'due to multi-head formulation'.

comments.title: Overstatement of MAM Adapter matching full fine-tuning across all tasks
comments.quote: MAM Adapter achieves comparable results to full fine-tuning by adding only 0.5% of pretrained parameters.
comments.explanation:
The paper claims MAM Adapter matches full fine-tuning on MNLI and SST2 with only 0.5% parameters. However, Table 2 shows Full-FT at 87.6±.4 (MNLI) and 94.6±.4 (SST2), while MAM Adapter is reported as 87.2±.3 and 94.2±.2—both slightly below full fine-tuning. The difference is small (0.4 and 0.4 points), but not 'comparable' in a strict sense, especially given the ± bars.

On XSum and MT, MAM Adapter reaches 45.06/21.90/36.87 and 37.5, vs full fine-tuning at 44.81/21.94/36.83 and 37.3—so R-2 is nearly matched, but R-1 and BLEU exceed it. However, the full fine-tuning numbers are from 'our run' and lower than reported in prior work (†), suggesting possible suboptimal full fine-tuning.

The claim 'matches full fine-tuning' is therefore context-dependent. On some metrics it matches, on others it exceeds, but never clearly surpasses all. Given the sensitivity of full fine-tuning to hyperparameters (noted in footnote), the comparison may reflect differences in tuning rather than superiority of MAM Adapter. The conclusion overstates the evidence, especially for classification tasks where it consistently trails.

comments.title: Inconsistent spelling of 'Prefix tuning' as 'Prefx' or 'Prefx tuning'
comments.quote: Prefx tuning (Li & Liang, 2021),_l_=200|3.6%
comments.explanation:
The term 'Prefix tuning' is repeatedly misspelled as 'Prefx tuning' (missing 'i') in multiple tables and text (Table 2, Table 6, Figure 2 caption). This appears in both the main text and tables. While likely due to OCR or typesetting error (given 'f' and 'i' being close), the same error appears consistently across instances.

However, given the instruction to consider OCR errors and flag only if a plausible intended symbol would still be an issue, this spelling inconsistency is not a technical or logical flaw. It's clearly a typo for 'Prefix', which is correctly spelled elsewhere (e.g., §2.2, §3.1). The meaning is unambiguous.

Thus, although visually distracting, it does not mislead or affect understanding. Per the instructions, such formatting issues should not be flagged.

comments.title: Ambiguous Training Time Claim for MAM Adapter
comments.quote: the final MAM Adapter needs 100% - 150% training time of full fine-tuning in our four benchmarks since parameter-efficient tuning typically needs more epochs to converge
comments.explanation: At first, I interpreted this statement as claiming that the MAM Adapter's total training time is 1 to 1.5 times that of full fine-tuning. However, the justification—that parameter-efficient methods need more epochs—suggests that the increased time is due to longer training schedules, not more expensive per-epoch computation. This is plausible, as many PEFT methods require more epochs to converge. However, the statement is ambiguous: it could be read as implying that the per-epoch cost is similar (just more epochs), but the phrase 'needs 100% - 150% training time' lacks clarity on whether this is wall-clock time, FLOPs, or includes overhead from extra parameters. Given that MAM combines prefix tuning (attn, l=30) and scaled PA (ffn, r=512), which add nontrivial computation, especially in the decoder layers for sequence generation, the per-epoch cost might exceed full fine-tuning. The authors correctly note that inference time is similar, but the training time claim is underspecified. They should clarify whether the 100–150% refers to total wall-clock time and confirm that per-epoch computational overhead is negligible compared to the base model. As it stands, a reader might conclude the method is computationally neutral during training, which may not be accurate.

comments.title: Lack of Specification for Renewable Energy Impact
comments.quote: Notably, the great majority of the experimentation done for this paper was performed on a data center powered entirely by renewable energy.
comments.explanation: The sentence is clearly intended to acknowledge environmental responsibility, which is commendable. However, as a claim about environmental impact, it raises questions about completeness.
